author: @jamest.5001
text: I been debating on weather to keep the electromagnet , or replace the coil with a thick ring magnet, and reassemble the rotor ,  I was thinking about making an aluminum, or even epoxy wood composite ring, filled with strong neodymium magnets,. I'd like to use 3 60-100amp alternators,  with. A decent size 3-5blade turbine, if I can get 500watts from a breezy day, and maybe 500-1500w on a stormy day, with 20mph winds, it would be awesome,  but 2/3 would be fairly good, I'm thinking about using 1.5"-2" angle , 3 pieces parallel, with 1/4" to 3/8" bar welded to create a tower, 40' tall, going from 18" spacing, to 8" spacing at the top, with the bars bracing around every 16" with a 15-20' section of 1.5" pipe to mount the turbines on, mount one at the top, if the pipe, and another about 5' above the tower , at the 5' height of the pipe, running 2x 3phase to the base, to a rectifier, so they can be series /paralleled, depending on. The wind available, maybe it will work, maybe not, but I'm hoping to atleast get 50-100w per, in a normal breeze, near 500w on a more windy day, but mostly to makeup for sunless days, when it tends to be windy,

author: @jima3252
text: Nice setup Rob. I would guess you have about a 1 to 16 ratio there, if so then the alternator is spinning at about 1000 rpm or more. It can put out 50 amps or more at 14 volts with a voltage regulator, so about 700 watts. Without a voltage regulator who knows what voltage it might go to before the diodes burn out from the current. It would depend on the load resistance. A proper fuse could help prevent that as well. Since you have plenty of amps, you can just measure output volts. Then take volts squared / ohms of prospective load to estimate watts. Or take volts / ohms of potential load to estimate amps to select a fuse to protect load and alternator. Cheers.

author: @Staedford
text: Great, would love to have seen you turn the crank with a torque wrench, to compare torque v field current. One of the big killers of PMA systems is the high cogging as a result of constant high torque. I don't understand why variable field systems are not more widely available.

author: @jonesPossibly
text: Can someone explain why you put a load on it?  sorry, my electronic knowledge is very sparse.

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: Here's a hack at it: A load is resistance.  Ohms law says (my bad phrasing) that power (in watts) is current (amps) divided by resistance (in ohms).  One cannot divide by zero so a current over a perfect conductor with no load cannot have a system power greater than zero.  Likewise 0 current divided by any load value is also 0 power.

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text:
power=Ohm x squared Ampère
power=Volt x Ampère

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @hommerdalor6301 Much better than my poor memory, thanks.  Revised: Power is volts times amps.  Current is volts divided by resistance.   No resistance = no current, and no works gets done.

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @jonesPossibly My memory failed me -- see reply to hommer dalor.  Principle is correct but I labeled the bits incorrectly.
